title: Tooltip vs popover vs inline message vs icon-only ambiguous action
summary: Use a tooltip for short noninteractive supplemental descriptions on focus or hover, a popover for small anchored controls or richer context, an inline message for persistent important guidance, and labelled controls instead of relying on mystery icons.
decisionRules: Choose tooltip when a focusable trigger already has a usable name and only needs a brief supplemental description that can appear on focus or hover and disappear without changing state.
Choose popover when the anchored layer needs buttons, links, form controls, choices, a title, or user-driven open and close behavior.
Choose inline message when the information is required, time-sensitive, validation-related, or must remain visible while the user completes the task.
Choose visible labels or labelled icon buttons when the user cannot predict the action before activation; do not make tooltip text the only action name.
Use aria-describedby from the trigger to the tooltip text when the tooltip is meant to describe, not rename, the target.
Do not put links, inputs, buttons, menus, or other focusable controls inside a tooltip because focus stays on the trigger.
If the content needs more than a short sentence, has structure, or must be read after focus moves, make it inline help, details disclosure, or a popover instead.
If a disabled control needs explanation, make the explanation reachable without requiring focus on a disabled element that cannot receive keyboard focus.
A tooltip may supplement an icon-only control, but the accessible name and visible affordance must still be clear before activation.
Escape should hide the tooltip without moving focus from the triggering control.
failureModes: A destructive icon has no accessible name and depends on a hover-only tooltip that touch and keyboard users may never see.
A tooltip contains a Learn more link, but focus cannot enter the tooltip because the trigger still owns focus.
Required password rules are hidden in a tooltip, so users lose the instructions while typing.
A disabled button's only explanation appears on hover even though keyboard users cannot focus the disabled control.
A long policy explanation is squeezed into a tiny tooltip instead of inline help or a popover.
Pressing Escape hides the tooltip but also moves focus away from the control, breaking the user's place in the toolbar.

sources.note: APG supports focus and hover triggering, focus remaining on the trigger, and Escape dismissal.
MDN supports aria-describedby ownership and the no-interactive-content boundary.
Fluent supports concise supplemental content, placement, arrows, and disabled-control explanation guidance.
Red Hat supports the Tab and Escape keyboard contract for showing and hiding tooltips.
